name: Dr Christopher Gerteis
title: Senior Lecturer in the History of Contemporary Japan | Academic Staff, Japan Research Centre
department: School of Languages, Cultures and Linguistics
expertise_topics: Christopher's research focuses on the social and cultural history of Japan since the nineteenth century
emphasizing how class
gender

and generation shaped the formation of political consciousness and collective identity. His major publications include Gender Struggles: Wage-Earning Women and Male-Dominated Unions in Postwar Japan (Harvard
which won Second Prize in the European Association for Japanese Studies Book Award
and Mobilizing Japanese Youth: The Cold War and the Making of the Sixties Generation (Cornell
which received the SOAS Best Book Award in 2022. His next book
『戦後青年と過激主義-冷戦下の政治意識』 [Postwar Youth and Radicalism: Political Consciousness during the Cold War]
is in press with the University of Tokyo Press and scheduled for publication in 2026
His recent work includes the article 「選択的記憶と戦略的忘却：日本における歴史の商品化と産業遺産」 ["Selective Memory and Strategic Forgetting: The Commodification of History and Industrial Heritage in Japan"]
published in the 『東洋文化研究所紀要』 [Bulletin of the Institute of Oriental Culture] (vol. 186
2024). This line of research builds on his role as co-PI of the Hashima XR Project
which used Extended Reality technologies to recreate the Hashima coal mining community (Gunkanjima). Further publications on this project are forthcoming
His book-in-progress
Japan's Unfinished Empire: Colonies
Capital
and the Afterlives of Imperialism
situates the Japanese empire as constitutive of modern state formation rather than as an aberration or reactive policy. The project foregrounds settler colonialism
transregional labor
and administrative logics that linked domestic reform to colonial governance
emphasizing the recursive "scaled repetition" of imperial techniques across domestic and colonial spaces
Alongside this
he is developing new work in the computational humanities. He publishes Past Meets Pixel @ Substack
exploring the intersection of history and video games
and Japanese Modernity @ Substack
focusing on historiography
memory
and global contexts. Both platforms foster collaborations among scholars
developers
and educators in rethinking how historical knowledge is produced and shared
research_interests: Christopher's research focuses on the social and cultural history of Japan since the nineteenth century; emphasizing how class; gender; and generation shaped the formation of political consciousness and collective identity. His major publications include Gender Struggles: Wage-Earning Women and Male-Dominated Unions in Postwar Japan (Harvard; 2010); which won Second Prize in the European Association for Japanese Studies Book Award; and Mobilizing Japanese Youth: The Cold War and the Making of the Sixties Generation (Cornell; 2021); which received the SOAS Best Book Award in 2022. His next book; 『戦後青年と過激主義-冷戦下の政治意識』 [Postwar Youth and Radicalism: Political Consciousness during the Cold War]; is in press with the University of Tokyo Press and scheduled for publication in 2026; His recent work includes the article 「選択的記憶と戦略的忘却：日本における歴史の商品化と産業遺産」 ["Selective Memory and Strategic Forgetting: The Commodification of History and Industrial Heritage in Japan"]; published in the 『東洋文化研究所紀要』 [Bulletin of the Institute of Oriental Culture] (vol. 186; 2024). This line of research builds on his role as co-PI of the Hashima XR Project; which used Extended Reality technologies to recreate the Hashima coal mining community (Gunkanjima). Further publications on this project are forthcoming; His book-in-progress; Japan's Unfinished Empire: Colonies; Capital; and the Afterlives of Imperialism; situates the Japanese empire as constitutive of modern state formation rather than as an aberration or reactive policy. The project foregrounds settler colonialism; transregional labor; and administrative logics that linked domestic reform to colonial governance; emphasizing the recursive "scaled repetition" of imperial techniques across domestic and colonial spaces; Alongside this; he is developing new work in the computational humanities. He publishes Past Meets Pixel @ Substack; exploring the intersection of history and video games; and Japanese Modernity @ Substack; focusing on historiography; memory; and global contexts. Both platforms foster collaborations among scholars; developers; and educators in rethinking how historical knowledge is produced and shared
